prd.name: Static Read-only Export
prd.overview: A way to publish the PRD dashboard as a read-only static site (e.g. on GitHub Pages) without running the Python backend. A CLI command writes the same JSON payloads the live server would serve, and the frontend has a static mode in which it reads those payloads instead of calling the API, hides every mutation control, and surfaces a "Read-only" indicator. The feature exists so consumer repos that only want to publish their PRDs to a browsable site (e.g. https://<org>.github.io/<repo>/) can do it with a short CI workflow rather than reimplementing snapshot logic or patching the dashboard.

requirement.name: export-json CLI command
requirement.description: A new prd export-json <out> subcommand writes the dashboard's data to disk in the same shape the live API serves, so a static host can replace the backend.
requirement.rule: prd export-json resolves the PRD root the same way discovery/root-and-refs defines it, and exits non-zero with a clear error if no root is found.
The command writes <out>/index.json whose payload matches what GET /api/index would return for the same PRD root.

For every <module>/<feature>.xml under the PRD root (excluding index.xml), the command writes <out>/prd/<module>/<feature>.json whose payload matches what GET /api/prd/<module>/<feature> would return.

Every non-XML file under each module directory is copied to <out>/asset/<module>/<relative-path>, preserving directory structure, so images referenced from PRD rich content resolve under the static host.
The output directory is created if it does not exist; existing files in it are overwritten without prompting.
On success the command prints a one-line summary naming the count of features and assets written and the absolute output path.
The command never writes to any file under the PRD root; only the output directory is touched.

requirement.name: Static frontend mode
requirement.description: When built with the static-export environment variable set, the dashboard fetches its data from the exporter's JSON tree instead of the live API and presents itself as read-only. The behavior of the live, server-backed dashboard described in dashboard/viewer is unchanged when the variable is unset.
requirement.rule: Setting VITE_STATIC_BASE at build time switches the dashboard into static mode; leaving it unset keeps the existing live-server behavior. The value of VITE_STATIC_BASE is the URL prefix where the exporter's output is served.
The index view fetches <VITE_STATIC_BASE>/index.json instead of /api/index.
The feature detail view fetches <VITE_STATIC_BASE>/prd/<module>/<feature>.json instead of /api/prd/<module>/<feature>.
Relative values inside PRD rich content rewrite to /asset//; absolute and data URLs pass through unchanged.
The dashboard does not open an EventSource; the "live updates disconnected" banner from the live dashboard never appears.
The rule, bug, and finding mutation APIs reject with a read-only error if invoked, so any stray call surfaces as a clear failure rather than silently corrupting cache state.

requirement.name: Read-only UX
requirement.description: In static mode every control that would write back to the PRD is removed and the dashboard makes its read-only state visible.
requirement.rule: A rule's status glyph is rendered as a static indicator with no click target; the cycle-status button from the live dashboard's lightweight edits is not shown.
The "advance bug status" button on each bug card is hidden; bugs are still readable with all their fields.
The "Resolve" button on each UI-review finding is hidden; findings are still readable.
The masthead shows a small "Read-only" chip alongside the publication eyebrow, so a visitor immediately understands the site cannot be edited.
Sidebar navigation, feature deep-links, fixed-bug fold, and figma-node chips all behave the same as in the live dashboard.

requirement.name: GitHub Pages compatibility
requirement.description: The static build is deployable as-is to GitHub Pages, including project pages served under a repository subpath where deep links and asset URLs would otherwise break.
requirement.rule: Setting VITE_BASE at build time scopes every emitted asset URL to that public path, so a site served from https://<org>.github.io/<repo>/ resolves its CSS and JS bundles correctly.
React Router's basename is derived from the build's base path, so deep links like /<repo>/p/<module>/<feature> match the feature route instead of falling through to the 404 view.
The build emits a 404.html identical to index.html so GitHub Pages' not-found handler boots the SPA and the router takes over from the requested URL.
The build emits a .nojekyll marker so Pages' Jekyll layer does not filter any of Vite's output filenames.
The static build writes to frontend/dist instead of the wheel's bundled static/ directory, so a static-host deployment never overwrites the assets the Python package ships.
